blocks:
Ayahs

عَبَسَ وَتَوَلَّىٰٓ (١)
He frowned and turned away (1)
أَن جَآءَهُ ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ (٢)
when the blind man approached him. (2)
وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّهُۥ يَزَّكَّىٰٓ (٣)
And how do you know, maybe he would purify himself, (3)
أَوْ يَذَّكَّرُ فَتَنفَعَهُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰٓ (٤)
or take admonition, and the admonition would benefit him! (4)
أَمَّا مَنِ ٱسْتَغْنَىٰ (٥)
But as for someone who is wealthy, (5)
فَأَنتَ لَهُۥ تَصَدَّىٰ (٦)
you attend to him, (6)
وَمَا عَلَيْكَ أَلَّا يَزَّكَّىٰ (٧)
though you are not liable if he does not purify himself. (7)
وَأَمَّا مَن جَآءَكَ يَسْعَىٰ (٨)
But as for someone who comes hurrying to you, (8)
وَهُوَ يَخْشَىٰ (٩)
while he fears [Allah], (9)
فَأَنتَ عَنْهُ تَلَهَّىٰ (١٠)
you are neglectful of him. (10)

Content of Surah "Abasa"

Despite its brevity, Surah "Abasa" discusses various important issues and places a particular emphasis on the matter of the "Resurrection." Its content can be summarized into five topics:

  1. Allah’s severe rebuke toward one who did not behave appropriately toward a truth-seeking blind man.
  2. The value and importance of the Holy Quran.
  3. Man’s ingratitude and ungratefulness toward Allah’s blessings.
  4. Mentioning a portion of His blessings regarding the nourishment of humans and animals to stimulate man's sense of gratitude.
  5. Referring to soul-shaking parts of the events of the Day of Judgment and the fate of the believers and disbelievers on that Great Day.

Its naming is based on the first verse of the Surah.

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Abasa' will arrive at the gathering place on the Day of Resurrection while his face is laughing and joyful." [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 435.

In the Name of Allah, the Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful

عَبَسَ وَ تَوَلَّى (1) أَنْ جاءَهُ الْأَعْمى‌ (2) وَ ما يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّهُ يَزَّكَّى (3) أَوْ يَذَّكَّرُ فَتَنْفَعَهُ الذِّكْرى‌ (4) أَمَّا مَنِ اسْتَغْنى‌ (5) فَأَنْتَ لَهُ تَصَدَّى (6) وَ ما عَلَيْكَ أَلاَّ يَزَّكَّى (7) وَ أَمَّا مَنْ جاءَكَ يَسْعى‌ (8) وَ هُوَ يَخْشى‌ (9) فَأَنْتَ عَنْهُ تَلَهَّى (10)

Occasion of Revelation

These verses generally indicate that Allah has rebuked someone in them for prioritizing a wealthy individual or individuals over a truth-seeking blind man. But who is this person being rebuked? There is a difference of opinion regarding this:

The famous view among both Sunni and Shi'a commentators is that: A group of Quraysh leaders, such as Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, Abu Jahl, Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, and others, were with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet was busy preaching and inviting them toward Islam. He hoped that these words would be effective in their hearts (and certainly, if such individuals accepted Islam, they would draw another group toward Islam, and their obstructions would cease, benefiting Islam from both directions). Meanwhile, "Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum," who was a blind and apparently poor man, entered the gathering.

He requested the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to recite verses of the Quran for him and teach him, and he repeatedly echoed his request and would not remain quiet, as he did not precisely realize with whom the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was busy talking.

He interrupted the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله speech so much that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became annoyed, and signs of displeasure appeared on his noble face, and he said to himself: "These Arab leaders will say to themselves that the followers of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله are the blind and the slaves." Therefore, he turned away from "Abdullah" and continued his conversation with that group.

At this moment, the above verses were revealed (rebuking the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in this regard). After this incident, the Messenger صلى الله عليه وآله always held "Abdullah" in high esteem, and whenever he saw him, he would say: "Welcome to the one for whom my Lord rebuked me," and then he would ask him: "Do you have a need that I may fulfill?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله even appointed him as his deputy in Medina twice during Islamic battles. [^1]

The second occasion of revelation narrated for the above verses is that they were revealed concerning a man from the "Umayyad" clan who was sitting with the Prophet. At that moment, "Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum" entered. When the man's eyes fell upon "Abdullah," he pulled himself together as if he feared becoming contaminated, frowned, and turned his face away. Allah recounted his action in the above verses and subjected him to blame and reproach. This occasion of revelation is narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. ^2 The great Shi'a researcher, the late "Sayyid Murtada," has accepted this occasion of revelation.

[^1] and ^2: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 437.

Of course, there is nothing in the verse that explicitly indicates the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself is intended. The only thing that could serve as a contextual clue for this meaning is the direct address found in verses 8 to 10 of this Surah, which say: "But as for him who comes to you striving [for the truth], and he fears [Allah], from him you are distracted!" This is something that could apply to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله more appropriately than anyone else.

However, as the late "Sayyid Murtada" stated, there are also clues in these verses indicating that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself is not intended, including: "Frowning" is not among the attributes of the Prophets, especially the Prophet of Islam. He spoke with a cheerful face even toward his enemies, let alone toward truth-seeking believers.

Furthermore, attending to the wealthy and neglecting the truth-seeking poor does not align with his character, which is referred to in verse 4 of Surah "Nun": وَ إِنَّكَ لَعَلى‌ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ: "And indeed, you are of a great moral character" (especially since it is well-known that Surah "Nun" was revealed before Surah "Abasa"). [^1]

But even assuming the first occasion of revelation is the reality, this matter is nothing more than a "leaving of the better" (Tark al-Awla), and nothing is observed in it that contradicts the station of Infallibility (Ismah).

Because, firstly, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had no goal other than influencing the leaders of Quraysh, spreading the call of Islam through this path, and breaking their resistance.

[^1]: Same source.

Secondly, frowning in front of a blind man does not cause a problem because he cannot see. Additionally, Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum had also not observed the etiquette of the gathering, for when he heard the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was busy talking with a group, he should not have interrupted his speech.

However, since Allah gives extraordinary importance to kindness and gentleness toward the oppressed truth-seeking believers, He does not approve of even this much indifference toward this believing man from His Prophet and rebukes him. All of this is on one hand.

On the other hand, if we look at these verses from the perspective of the truthfulness and greatness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we see they are on the level of a miracle. For the great leader of Islam mentions such a responsibility for himself in his Divine Book that he sees even the smallest "leaving of the better"—meaning a slight indifference toward a truth-seeking blind man—rebuked by Allah. This is living proof that this Divine Book is from Allah and that he is a truthful Prophet. Certainly, if this book were not from Allah, it would not contain such content.

And even more amazing is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, according to the narration mentioned above, whenever he saw "Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum," he would remember this incident and respect him greatly.

Furthermore, these verses can express the Islamic culture in dealing with the oppressed (Mustad'afin) and the arrogant (Mustakbirin)—how it prioritizes a poor, blind, believing man over all those wealthy and powerful leaders of the polytheist Arabs. This clearly shows that Islam is the supporter of the oppressed and the enemy of the arrogant.

At the end of this discussion, we repeat once more that although the first occasion of revelation is the most famous among commentators, it must be admitted that there is nothing in the verse itself that explicitly proves the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is intended.

Commentary

A Severe Rebuke for Indifference Toward a Truth-Seeking Blind Man

With attention to what was said regarding the occasion of revelation, we turn to the commentary of the verses.

First, He says: "He frowned and turned away," (عَبَسَ وَ تَوَلَّى).

"Because the blind man came to him." (أَنْ جاءَهُ الْأَعْمى‌).

"But what would make you know? Perhaps he might seek to purify himself," (وَ ما يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّهُ يَزَّكَّى‌).

"Or be reminded, and the reminder would benefit him." (أَوْ يَذَّكَّرُ فَتَنْفَعَهُ الذِّكْرى‌).

And if he does not become one hundred percent pure and pious, at least he will take heed from the reminder and awaken, and this awakening will have a general effect on him. [^1]

Then He continues this rebuke, adding: "As for him who considers himself self-sufficient," (أَمَّا مَنِ اسْتَغْنى‌).

"To him you give attention!" (فَأَنْتَ لَهُ تَصَدَّى‌). And you are persistent in guiding him while he is trapped in the arrogance of wealth and selfishness—an arrogance that is the source of rebellion and defiance, just as it appears in verses 6-7 of Surah al-Alaq: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى‌ أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى‌ "Indeed, man transgresses because he sees himself self-sufficient." ^2

"While it is no blame upon you if he does not purify himself." (وَ ما عَلَيْكَ أَلَّا يَزَّكَّى‌).

Your duty is only to convey the message, whether they take heed or feel weary. Therefore, for the sake of such individuals, you cannot ignore or grieve a truth-seeking blind man, even if your goal is the guidance of these defiant ones.

Once again, He resumes the emphasis and rebuke, still in the form of a direct address, saying: "But as for him who comes to you striving," (وَ أَمَّا مَنْ جاءَكَ يَسْعى‌).

"And he fears [Allah]," (وَ هُوَ يَخْشى‌) [^1] and this very motive of fearing Allah has sent him after you, so that he may hear more truths and apply them, and purify himself.

"From him you are distracted [to another]." (فَأَنْتَ عَنْهُ تَلَهَّى‌) ^2 The use of "Anta" (You) is actually an indication that someone like you should not be distracted for a moment from such a truth-seeking person to attend to another.

[^1]: Therefore, the difference between this verse and the previous one is that there the talk is of complete purity and piety, and here the talk is of the general impact of the reminder, even if it does not reach the station of complete piety; consequently, that truth-seeking blind man benefits from the reminders, whether it be a complete benefit or a brief benefit. It is also understood from some commentators that the difference between the two is that the first refers to being purified from sins and the second verse refers to acquiring acts of obedience, but the first interpretation seems more correct.

^2: "Raghib" says in "Mufradat": "Ghani," "Istaghna," "Taghanna," and "Taghani" have the same meaning. Also, according to him, "Tasadda" is from the root "Sada" (rhyming with Fata), which originally means the sound that returns from a mountain (in Persian it is called "Pajvak" [echo]). Then the word "Tasadda" came to be used in the sense of facing something and giving it full attention.

[^1]: Without doubt, the meaning of "Khashyat" here is fear of Allah—a fear that sends a person to investigate further, similar to what theologians have said regarding the necessity of knowing Allah based on averting potential harm. The possibility Fakhr Razi mentioned—that it means fear of the disbelievers or fear of falling due to blindness—is very far-fetched.

Even if your goal in attending to others is their guidance, because in the calculation of priorities, the priority belongs to this group of pure-hearted oppressed people.

In any case, this rebuke and address, whether to the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or someone else, expresses this important reality: that Islam and the Quran hold a special importance and respect for the seekers of the path of truth, especially from the oppressed classes.

Conversely, it takes a sharp and harsh stance against those who, due to the abundance of Divine blessings, have become intoxicated and arrogant—to the extent that Allah is not pleased that for the sake of attending to them, the slightest offense should occur toward this truth-seeking oppressed class.

The reason for this is also clear, for this group has always been the sincere supporters of Islam and the helpers of the great leaders of religion in difficulties, and the self-sacrificing ones on the fields of battle and martyrdom. As the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in the famous command to Malik al-Ashtar:

"The pillar of the religion, the gathering of the Muslims, and the prepared force against the enemies are only the common people of the nation; so let your listening be for them and your inclination be with them." [^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha: Section of Letters, Letter 53.

Ayahs

كَلَّآ إِنَّهَا تَذْكِرَةٌ (١١)
No indeed! These [verses of the Quran ] are a reminder (11)
فَمَن شَآءَ ذَكَرَهُۥ (١٢)
—so let anyone who wishes remember— (12)
فِى صُحُفٍ مُّكَرَّمَةٍ (١٣)
in honoured scriptures, (13)
مَّرْفُوعَةٍ مُّطَهَّرَةٍۭ (١٤)
exalted and purified, (14)
بِأَيْدِى سَفَرَةٍ (١٥)
in the hands of envoys, (15)
كِرَامٍۭ بَرَرَةٍ (١٦)
noble and pious. (16)
قُتِلَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَآ أَكْفَرَهُۥ (١٧)
Perish man! How ungrateful is he! (17)
مِنْ أَىِّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقَهُۥ (١٨)
From what did He create him? (18)
مِن نُّطْفَةٍ خَلَقَهُۥ فَقَدَّرَهُۥ (١٩)
He created him from a drop of [seminal] fluid; then proportioned him. (19)
ثُمَّ ٱلسَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُۥ (٢٠)
Then He made the way easy for him; (20)
ثُمَّ أَمَاتَهُۥ فَأَقْبَرَهُۥ (٢١)
then He made him die and buried him; (21)
ثُمَّ إِذَا شَآءَ أَنشَرَهُۥ (٢٢)
and then, when He wished, resurrected him. (22)
كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ مَآ أَمَرَهُۥ (٢٣)
No indeed! He has not yet carried out what He has commanded him. (23)

Commentary

Only the Pure May Reach the Quran

Following the previous verses which rebuked the one who showed indifference toward a truth-seeking blind man, these verses address the significance of the Holy Quran, its pure origin, and its impact on souls, stating:

"Never repeat such an act and forget it forever" (كَلَّا).

"For these verses are a means of reminder and admonition for God's creation" (إِنَّها تَذْكِرَةٌ) [^1]. There is no need for you to neglect the pure-hearted downtrodden and turn toward the influential and arrogant.

It is also possible that the phrase "كَلَّا إِنَّها تَذْكِرَةٌ" is a response to all the slanders of the polytheists and enemies of Islam regarding the Quran—who sometimes called it poetry, sometimes magic, and sometimes a form of soothsaying. The Quran says: none of these attributions are correct; rather, these verses are a means for awakening, remembrance, awareness, and faith, and the proof of this lies within the Quran itself. For anyone who approaches it—except for the stubborn antagonists—feels this effect within themselves.

Then He adds: "So whoever wills may take heed from it" (فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ) [^2]. This expression is both an indication that there is no compulsion or coercion involved, and a proof of the freedom of human will; until one desires and decides to accept guidance, he cannot benefit from the verses of the Quran.

[^1]: The feminine pronoun "انها" (it/they) refers to the verses of the Quran. [^2]: The pronoun in the phrase "ذكره" refers to the same thing as the pronoun "انها," except that there the intent was the "verses" (plural/feminine), while here it refers to the "Quran" itself, which is grammatically masculine.

Then He adds: "These great Divine words are recorded in scrolls (tablets and pages) of great value" (فِي صُحُفٍ مُكَرَّمَةٍ).

"صُحُف" (Suhuf) is the plural of "صحيفة" (Sahifah), meaning a tablet, a page, or anything upon which something is written. This expression shows that the Quranic verses, before being revealed to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, were written on tablets brought by the angels of revelation—tablets that are highly esteemed and precious.

The opinion of some who say these "صُحُف" refer to the books of previous prophets does not seem consistent with the preceding and succeeding verses. Similarly, the view that it refers to the "Preserved Tablet" (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz) also seems inappropriate, as the word "صُحُف" is used in the plural form, which is not typically used for the Preserved Tablet.

Then He says: "These scrolls and tablets are exalted and purified" (مَرْفُوعَةٍ مُطَهَّرَةٍ). They are too high for the hands of the unworthy to reach, or for anyone to be capable of distorting them. They are too pure for the hands of the impure to defile them, and they are also pure from any contradiction, conflict, doubt, or uncertainty.

Furthermore, "These Divine verses are in the hands of scribes/ambassadors" (بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ).

"Ambassadors who are high-ranking, obedient, submissive, and righteous" (كِرامٍ بَرَرَةٍ). "سفرة" (Safarah - rhyming with talabah) is the plural of "سافر" (Safar), derived from the root "سفر" (Safar - rhyming with qamar), which originally means to uncover or unveil something. Therefore, an ambassador who travels between peoples to solve their problems and unveil ambiguities is called a "سفير" (Safir). A writer is also called a "سافر" because he unveils the meaning of a matter.

Thus, the meaning of "سفرة" here refers to the Divine angels who are the ambassadors of revelation or the scribes of His verses.

Some have said that "سفرة" here refers to the huffaz (memorizers), reciters, and scribes of the Quran, and the scholars who protect these verses in every age and time from the interference of devils.

However, this interpretation seems unlikely because these verses speak of the time of the revelation's descent and the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, not the future.

Yet, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "The one who memorizes the Quran and acts upon it will be with the noble and righteous ambassadors (angels)." [^1] This expression clearly shows that the memorizers, commentators, and practitioners of the Quran are in the same rank as these ambassadors and move alongside them, not that they are the ambassadors themselves. It is a reality that when these scholars and memorizers perform a task similar to that of the angels and carriers of revelation, they are placed in their rank.

In any case, from the sum of this discourse, it is understood that all those who strive to preserve and revive the Quran hold an exalted station similar to the station of the "noble and righteous" angels.

"كِرام" (Kiram) is the plural of "كريم" (Karim), meaning "noble and honorable," referring to the greatness of the angels of revelation in the presence of Allah and their high status. Sometimes it is said this expression refers to their purity from any sin, as mentioned in verses 26-27 of Surah Al-Anbiya describing the angels: "Nay, they are honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command."

"بررة" (Bararah) is the plural of "بار" (Barr - like talib and talabah), derived from the root "بر" (Birr), which originally means vastness and expansiveness. Thus, vast deserts are called "بَر" (Barr). Since righteous people have an expansive existence and their blessings reach others, they are called "بار" (Barr).

Of course, the meaning of righteousness in the verse under discussion is obedience to the Divine command and purity from sin.

Thus, Allah has described the angels with three attributes: First, they are His ambassadors and carriers of revelation. Second, they are inherently noble and precious. Third, their actions are pure, characterized by obedience, submission, and righteousness.

Then He adds: Despite all these means of Divine guidance revealed in honored scrolls through the archangels of God with various reminders, this rebellious and ungrateful human still does not submit to the Truth. "Woe to man! How ungrateful he is!" (قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ ما أَكْفَرَهُ) [^1].

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 438. [^1]: The expression "قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ" (May man be killed/perished) is a form of curse, and according to Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, it is one of the severest types of curses. The "ما" in the phrase "ما أكفر" is for wonder/exclamation—wonder at one who, on one hand, has all these means of guidance at his disposal and, on the other hand, is covered by all these Divine blessings, yet still chooses the path of disbelief and ingratitude.

"كفر" (Kufr) here may mean lack of faith, or ingratitude, or any form of covering the truth. The comprehensive meaning is most suitable, as the preceding verses spoke of the means of guidance and faith, and the succeeding verses speak of the various blessings of the Lord.

In any case, the expression "Death to these individuals" is used metaphorically to express intense anger and loathing toward such disbelieving and ungrateful people.

Since the source of rebellion and ingratitude is often arrogance, to break this pride, the next verse asks: "From what thing did Allah create this man?" (مِنْ أَيِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلَقَهُ).

"He created him from a lowly and insignificant drop of fluid (nutfah), then proportioned him and determined his stages" (مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ خَلَقَهُ فَقَدَّرَهُ). Why does this human not reflect on the origin of his creation? Why does he forget the insignificance of his primary source? Furthermore, why does he not observe the power of Allah in creating this marvelous being from that tiny drop? Indeed, contemplating the creation of man from a drop and the measurement of all dimensions of his existence—his body parts, his talents, and his needs—is the best proof for recognizing God (Ma'rifatullah).

The word "قدّره" (qaddarahu) is from the root "تقدير" (taqdir), meaning to measure and proportion. We know that in the structure of human existence, more than twenty types of metals and semi-metals are used, each having a specific measure in terms of quantity and quality. If any increase or decrease occurs, the system of human existence collapses. Beyond this, the structural quality of body organs and their proportions and interconnections follow precise measurements. Likewise, the talents, instincts, and desires embedded in the individual and the collective human world must follow a specific calculation to ensure human happiness.

Allah is the One who performed all these measurements within that insignificant drop—a drop so small that if the primary life cells (spermatozoa) of all human beings were gathered in one place, they would not fill more than a thimble. Yes, in such a tiny entity, He drew and deposited all these wonders and designs.

Some have also interpreted "تقدير" as "preparing." It is also possible that "تقدير" refers to creating power within this tiny drop. How Great is that God who granted this weak creature such power and ability that he can make the heavens, the earth, and the depths of the seas his playground, and subjugate all the forces of his environment to his command! [^1] A combination of these three interpretations is also possible.

Continuing this discourse, He adds: "Then He made the path easy for him" (ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ). He made the path of evolutionary development of the fetus in the mother's womb easy, and then made the path of his transition into this world easy.

One of the wonders of human birth is that before the moments of birth, the fetus is positioned in the mother's womb with its head upward, its face toward the mother's back, and its feet in the lower part of the uterus. But when the command for birth is issued, it suddenly turns upside down; its head comes downward, and this very matter makes the process of birth easy for both the child and the mother. Of course, sometimes babies are born feet-first (breech), which creates many difficulties for the mother.

After birth, in the path of physical growth during childhood, then the development of instincts, and thereafter growth in the path of spiritual guidance and faith—He has made all of these easy for him through reason and the invitation of the prophets.

What a comprehensive and beautiful phrase it is, which, despite its brevity, refers to all these matters: "ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ."

It is also noteworthy that He says: "He made the path easy for man," and does not say: "He forced him to traverse this path." This is another emphasis on the issue of human free will and choice.

Then He refers to the final stage of human life after traversing this long path: "Then He caused him to die and put him in a grave" (ثُمَّ أَماتَهُ فَأَقْبَرَهُ). Certainly, "اماته" (causing death) is the work of God, but the matter of hiding him in a grave outwardly appears to be the work of man. However, since the intellect and intelligence necessary for this task, as well as other means, were provided by Allah, this act is attributed to Him.

Some have also said that attributing this act to God means that He created a grave for man, which is the heart of the earth. Others have considered this phrase to refer to the legislative decree and Divine command regarding the burial of the dead.

In any case, one of the honors Allah has bestowed upon the human race is this program of burying their bodies. For if there were no way to bury them, or if no command had been issued in this regard, and the human body remained on the ground to decay and then become prey for predatory animals and birds, it would be a strange humiliation. Therefore, Divine grace not only covers man during his life in the world but also includes him after death.

Furthermore, the command to bury the bodies of the dead (after washing, shrouding, and prayer) is an inspiring command indicating that the dead of humans must be pure and respected in every way—let alone the living! It is also noteworthy that the issue of death in this verse is listed among Divine blessings. If one reflects, it is indeed so, because firstly, death is a prelude to relief from the sufferings of this world and transition to the other abode, which is a much vaster world. Secondly, the death of existing generations makes room for future generations, allowing the human species to continue and evolve through generations; otherwise, the space would become so cramped for humans that living on earth would be impossible.

Interestingly, this same meaning is mentioned with a subtle indication in Surah Ar-Rahman, verses 26 to 28, where it says: "Everyone upon it will perish, and there remains the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor. So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" According to these verses, death is one of the great blessings of God.

Yes, the world with all its blessings is a prison for the believer, and exiting this world is being released from this prison. Moreover, the abundant Divine blessings sometimes become a source of negligence and heedlessness for a group, and the remembrance of death tears the veils of negligence; from this perspective, it is a blessing and a warning. Above all, if worldly life were to continue indefinitely, it would surely become tedious and exhausting, unlike the life of the Hereafter which is full of vitality and joy. I have grown weary of this two-day life, O Khidr! How do you manage, having an eternal life? [^1]

Then He addresses the stage of human resurrection, adding: "Then whenever He wills, He will resurrect him for account and recompense" (ثُمَّ إِذا شاءَ أَنْشَرَهُ). "انشره" (ansharahu) is from the root "انشار" (inshar), meaning to spread out after being folded or gathered. This is a beautiful expression showing that with death, human life is completely "folded," and in the resurrection, it is "spread out" in a larger and more exalted environment.

It is noteworthy that regarding dying and being hidden in the grave, the expression of "Divine will" is used implicitly, but here He says: "Whenever He wills, He will resurrect him." This difference in expression may be because no one knows the date of this great event (the Day of Judgment) except Him, whereas regarding death, it is generally known that man will inevitably die after living a natural lifespan.

In the final verse under discussion, He says: Despite all these Divine favors toward man—from the day he was an insignificant drop until the day he stepped into this world and traversed his path toward perfection, and then leaves this world and is hidden in the grave—this human still does not find his correct path. "No! He has not yet accomplished what He commanded him" (كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ ما أَمَرَهُ) [^1]. The use of "لما" (lamma), which is usually used for a negation coupled with expectation, indicates that with all these Divine favors and means of awakening provided to man, it is expected that he be serious and diligent in obeying Divine commands, yet it is surprising that he has not yet fulfilled this duty.

Regarding who is meant by "man" here, there are two interpretations: First, it refers to those humans who walk the path of disbelief, hypocrisy, denial of truth, oppression, and rebellion, as evidenced by verse 34 of Surah Ibrahim: "Indeed, man is [here] an extreme wrongdoer and ungrateful."

The second interpretation is that it includes all humans, for no one (whether believer or disbeliever) has fulfilled the right of servitude, worship, and obedience to God as is worthy of His Majestic station, as it has been said: It is best for the servant to bring an excuse For his shortcomings to the threshold of God, Otherwise, that which is worthy of His Lordship, No one is able to perform!

[^1]: The poet says: "I have grown weary of this two-day life, O Khidr! / How do you manage, having an eternal life?" [^1]: Some have interpreted "كلا" here to mean "truly," but it appears to have its famous meaning of "deterrence/negation" (rad'), because many short-sighted, boastful individuals imagine they have fulfilled their duties toward God. The verse says it is not as they think; they have not yet fulfilled their duty.

Ayahs

فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِهِۦٓ (٢٤)
Let man consider his food: (24)
أَنَّا صَبَبْنَا ٱلْمَآءَ صَبًّا (٢٥)
We pour down plenteous water [from the sky], (25)
ثُمَّ شَقَقْنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ شَقًّا (٢٦)
then We split the earth making fissures in it (26)
فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا حَبًّا (٢٧)
and make the grain grow in it, (27)
وَعِنَبًا وَقَضْبًا (٢٨)
as well as vines and vegetables, (28)
وَزَيْتُونًا وَنَخْلًا (٢٩)
olives and date palms, (29)
وَحَدَآئِقَ غُلْبًا (٣٠)
and densely-planted gardens, (30)
وَفَـٰكِهَةً وَأَبًّا (٣١)
fruits and pastures, (31)
مَّتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَـٰمِكُمْ (٣٢)
as a sustenance for you and your livestock. (32)

Commentary

Man Must Look at His Food!

Since the preceding verses spoke of the issue of the Resurrection, and the upcoming verses speak of this matter with even greater clarity, it appears that the verses under discussion serve as a proof for the Resurrection. They establish the possibility of the Rising by demonstrating Allah’s power over all things, as well as the revival of dead lands through the falling of rain—which is itself a type of "resurrection" in the plant kingdom.

Furthermore, since these verses speak of the various types of food that Allah has placed at the disposal of man and livestock, they stir man’s sense of gratitude and invite him to recognize the Provider and attain Ma'rifat Allah (knowledge of God).

First, He says: "Then let man look at his food," as to how Allah created it? (فَلْيَنْظُرِ الْإِنْسانُ إِلى‌ طَعامِهِ‌) [^1] The closest external objects to man are his food, which, through a transformation, becomes part of the fabric of his existence; if it does not reach him, he quickly heads toward annihilation. For this reason, the Quran has emphasized food items among all beings—specifically those derived from plants and trees.

It is clear that the meaning of "looking" is not a mere superficial glance, but rather looking with the meaning of contemplation and reflection upon the structure of these food substances, their life-giving components, the wondrous effects they have on man’s existence, and subsequently, reflecting upon their Creator.

The suggestion by some that this refers to a physical look—one that stimulates the salivary glands and aids digestion—seems very far-fetched. This is because the verse, in the context of the preceding and succeeding verses, is not at all concerned with explaining such matters. However, it is natural for some nutritionists, who view the Quran only through the limited lens of their personal field, to hold such a notion regarding the above verse.

Some also believe that looking at food means that when a person sits at the table, they should look closely at how it was obtained: through lawful (halal) or unlawful (haram) means? Legitimate or illegitimate? In this way, they should consider the ethical and legislative aspects.

In some narrations transmitted from the Infallibles (علیهم السلام), it is stated that "food" here refers to "knowledge and wisdom," which is the food of the human soul; one must look at whom they receive it from. Among these, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) that in the commentary of this verse he said: "His knowledge which he takes; from whom does he take it?" [^1] A similar meaning is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) as well. [^2] Undoubtedly, the literal meaning of the verse refers to physical food, which is described in detail in the following verses, but the food of the soul can also be inferred through the "argument of priority" (qiyas al-awlawiyyah). Since man is a composite of soul and body, just as his body

[^1]: It is possible that the phrase "let him look" is the result of an implied condition, such as: "If man is in doubt regarding his Lord and the Resurrection, then let him look at his food." [^1] and [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 429.

needs material food, his soul is also in need of spiritual food.

Where man must be meticulous regarding his physical food and recognize its source—which is the life-giving rain according to the subsequent verses—he must also exercise precision regarding his spiritual food, which descends through the rain of Revelation upon the land of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) pure heart. From there, it is stored in the chests of the Infallibles (علیهم السلام) and flows like gushing springs upon the pages of hearts, nurturing various delightful fruits of faith, piety, and moral virtues.

Indeed, man must look carefully to see where the primary source of his knowledge and wisdom—his spiritual food—lies, lest he be nourished from a contaminated source that sickens his soul or leads him to destruction.

Furthermore, through the argument of priority, the issue of "lawful and unlawful" and legitimate and illegitimate can be inferred from the verse by way of "implicatory indication" (dalalat al-iltizami).

It is also possible that both "food" in the noble verse has a broad and expansive meaning, as does "looking," and thus the three aforementioned interpretations are combined within it.

As for who is meant by "man" here, it goes without saying that it includes all human beings, whether believer or disbeliever. All must look at these food substances and the wonders and secrets hidden within them, so that the faithless may find the path of truth and the believers may increase in their faith.

Truly, every single food item—fruits, grains, vegetables—possesses a wondrous world of its own that can be studied for ages, yielding lessons that provide us with light and insight throughout our lives.

Then, He proceeds to a detailed description of these food substances and their sources, saying:

"For that We pour forth water in abundance," (أَنَّا صَبَبْنَا الْماءَ صَبًّا).

"Sabb" means "pouring water from above," and here it refers to the falling of rain. The expression "sabban" at the end of the verse is for emphasis and to indicate the abundance of this water.

Indeed, water, which is the most important element of life, is always sent down from the sky in great quantities by the grace of the Lord. We know that all rivers, springs, qanats, and wells draw their water reserves from the rain; the primary source of them all is rain, for "If rain does not fall on the mountains / In a year, the Tigris becomes a dry riverbed."

Therefore, when studying "food substances," man first notices the system of rainfall: how the sun constantly shines upon the surface of the seas, clouds rise from them, and winds set them in motion. Then, due to moving away from the earth's surface and entering the cold regions of the atmosphere, they turn back into water and fall—pure, clear water, free from any harmful minerals or pollution, in the form of small droplets or soft flakes of snow that settle gently on the earth and are absorbed by the soil, plants, and trees.

After mentioning water, which is one of the vital pillars for plant growth, He moves to the other vital pillar, namely "the earth," and adds: "Then We split the earth in fragments," (ثُمَّ شَقَقْنَا الْأَرْضَ شَقًّا).

Many commentators have said that this splitting refers to the splitting of the earth by plant sprouts. Truly, it is one of the wonders that a sprout, with all its softness and delicacy, splits the hard soil, and sometimes passes through rocks in the mountains to emerge. What great power has the Great Creator placed in this delicate sprout that it can display such might?!

Some have also suggested that it refers to the splitting of the earth through human plowing, or even by worms that perform a type of plowing along with other vital activities in the soil! It is true that plowing is the work of man, but since Allah has placed all the means for it at his disposal, it is attributed to Allah.

A third interpretation for this expression, which is preferable in some respects, is that the splitting of the earth refers to the breaking down of rocks on its surface.

To explain: In the beginning, the surface of the earth was covered by a massive layer of rock. Successive torrential rains fell and split the rocks, separated their particles, and spread them in the lowlands of the earth, thus forming the mass of arable soil. Even now, floods dissolve part of it and carry it to the sea, but new soil formed again by snow and rain takes its place; otherwise, man would suffer from a shortage of agricultural soil. In this way, the verse points to one of the scientific miracles of the Quran, showing that first the rains fall, and then the lands are split and become ready for cultivation—this process occurred not only in the early days but continues today.

This interpretation seems more appropriate since the issue of "inbat" (germination) and the growing of plants is mentioned in the following verse.

It is also not unlikely that all three interpretations are combined.

After mentioning these two fundamental pillars, namely "water" and "soil," He points to eight types of vegetation that are the basic pillars of food for humans or animals, saying: "And We cause the grain to grow therein," (فَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها حَبًّا).

Food grains are the primary source of nutrition for humans and various animals—grains which, if cut off for one year due to drought, would cause famine and hunger to encompass the entire world, and all humans would fall into hardship.

The use of "habban" in the indefinite form here serves to express the greatness or the diversity of these grains. The fact that some have interpreted it specifically as "wheat" and "barley" has no basis, as this expression includes all legumes and grains.

In the next stage, He adds: "And grapes and green fodder," (وَ عِنَباً وَ قَضْباً). The mention of "inab" (grapes) among all fruits is due to the abundant nutritional and vital substances hidden in this fruit, making it a complete food. (Note that "inab" refers to both the "grapes" and the "grapevine," and in the Quranic verses it is applied to both, but here "grapes" is more appropriate).

"Qadb" (rhyming with jadb) originally refers to vegetables that are harvested in several turns, and here it means various types of edible vegetables. Mentioning it after grapes is evidence of the importance of this food substance, which modern nutritional science emphasizes tremendously.

Sometimes the word "qadb" is used to mean cutting or picking, and the word "qadib" means a tree branch, and "sayf qadib" means a sharp sword.

It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that "qadb" here means "rutab" (fresh dates) which are picked from the tree, but this interpretation seems very unlikely, as the next verse makes a separate reference to the issue of dates.

Some have also suggested that "qadb" refers to vine-grown fruits (like cucumbers, watermelons, and the like) or root vegetables (like carrots, onions, and cabbage).

However, it is not unlikely that "qadb" here has a broad meaning that includes edible vegetables, vine-grown fruits, and food roots.

Then He adds: "And olives and palm trees," (وَ زَيْتُوناً وَ نَخْلًا). The emphasis on these two fruits is also for a clear reason, as it has been proven today that both "olives" and "dates" are among the most important energizing, beneficial, and health-promoting food substances.

In the next stage, He adds: "And gardens, dense with many trees," (وَ حَدائِقَ غُلْباً).

"Hada'iq" is the plural of "hadiqah," meaning a garden that is walled and protected. Originally, it means a piece of land that has water; the word is derived from "hadaqah" (the pupil of the eye) in which water (tears) constantly flows.

Since such gardens are usually fruit orchards, it could be an allusion to various types of heavenly fruits.

"Ghulb" (rhyming with qufl) is the plural of "aghlab" and "ghalba," meaning thick-necked. It is originally derived from the root ghalaba (victory/dominance), and here it means tall and sturdy trees.

Then He adds: "And fruits and herbage," (وَ فاكِهَةً وَ أَبًّا).

"Abb" (with a doubled ba) refers to wild plants and pastures ready for animals to graze or for plants to be harvested. Originally, it conveys the meaning of "readiness," and since such pastures are ready for utilization, they are called "abb."

Some have also said that "abb" refers to fruits that can be dried and stored for winter, because they are always ready for use.

Many commentators from both the Sunni and Shi'a schools have narrated under this verse that one day Umar was on the pulpit and recited these verses, then said: "All of these we know, but what is 'abb'? I do not know." Then he dropped the staff he had in his hand and said: "By Allah, this is a kind of affectation (takalluf); what harm is there if you do not know the meaning of 'abb'? Follow what has been made clear to you from the Book of Allah and act upon it, and what you do not know or understand, leave it to the Lord!" [^1]

This shows that he considered the concept of the word "abb" to be something complex, whereas referring to linguistic texts reveals that it is not a complex matter.

Surprisingly, it is mentioned in al-Durr al-Manthur that the same question was asked of Abu Bakr. He said: "Which sky would shade me and which earth would carry me if I were to say regarding the Book of Allah that which I do not know?"

It is true that many Sunni scholars have concluded from these two reports that no one should speak about matters they are not aware of, especially regarding the Book of Allah. However, the question remains: how can someone who wishes to rule as the leader and caliph of the Muslims be unaware of the linguistic meaning of a word that appears in the text of the Holy Quran and is not particularly complex? All of this is evidence that in every age and time, there must be a Divine leader among the people who is aware of all matters of the Sharia and is protected and infallible from every mistake and error.

Therefore, in a narration that the late al-Mufid recorded in al-Irshad, we read:

When this incident reached the ears of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), he said:

"Glory be to Allah! Did he not know that 'abb' is herbage and pasture? And that the words of the Almighty: 'And fruits and herbage' are an enumeration from Allah of His blessings upon His creation, in what He has nourished them with, and created for them and for their livestock, from that which gives life to their souls and sustains their bodies." [^1]

Here, a question arises: in the previous verses, some fruits were mentioned specifically, and here fruit is mentioned generally. Furthermore, the previous verse which spoke of gardens apparently referred to the fruits of the gardens; why is fruit mentioned again here?

In response, we say: As for why some fruits were mentioned specifically, like grapes, olives, and dates (by the mention of the palm tree), it is because of the extraordinary importance they hold among fruits. [^1] As for why "fakiha" (fruit) is mentioned separately from "hada'iq" (gardens), it may be because gardens have other benefits besides fruit: beautiful scenery, freshness, healthy air, and the like. Moreover, the leaves of some trees and the roots and bark of others are part of food items (like tea, cinnamon, ginger, and the like). Additionally, the leaves of many trees are suitable fodder for animals, and we know that what is mentioned in the above verses includes both human food and animal food.

Therefore, in the next verse, which is the last verse under discussion, He adds: "A provision for you and for your cattle," (مَتاعاً لَكُمْ وَ لِأَنْعامِكُمْ‌).

"Mata'" is anything from which man derives benefit and enjoyment.

Key Points Healthy Food Substances

What appeared in these verses were eight types of food substances for humans and livestock. Interestingly, all of them are plant-based and botanical foods. This is due to the importance that "plants," "grains," and "fruits" have in human nutrition; rather, these are the true food of man, while animal foods are in the second degree and in smaller quantities.

What is noteworthy here is that modern nutritional science—which is one of the vast and important sciences today, visualizing a vast world with even broader horizons—is in reality a commentary on what has appeared in these verses and reflects the greatness of the Quran. Specifically, the substances emphasized in the above verses are each very important and valuable from the perspective of nutritional science.

In any case, precision in these matters makes man more acquainted with the greatness of their Creator and His favors and love toward mankind.

Indeed, precision in the food of the body, and subsequently in the food of the soul—both in terms of content and structure, and in terms of the methods of acquisition—can advance man on the path of Ma'rifat Allah, purification of the soul, and self-building. Truly, "man must look at his food with precision," and how meaningful is this one short sentence.

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Qurtubi, Fi Zilal al-Quran, al-Durr al-Manthur, and al-Mizan under the verses discussed. [^1]: Irshad al-Mufid as quoted in al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 319. [^1]: We have provided a detailed discussion on the importance of olives, dates, and grapes as food substances in Vol. 11, p. 172 onwards (under Surah an-Nahl, verse 11).

Ayahs

فَإِذَا جَآءَتِ ٱلصَّآخَّةُ (٣٣)
So when the deafening Cry comes— (33)
يَوْمَ يَفِرُّ ٱلْمَرْءُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ (٣٤)
the day when a man will evade his brother, (34)
وَأُمِّهِۦ وَأَبِيهِ (٣٥)
his mother and his father, (35)
وَصَـٰحِبَتِهِۦ وَبَنِيهِ (٣٦)
his spouse and his sons— (36)
لِكُلِّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ (٣٧)
each of them will have a task to keep him preoccupied on that day. (37)
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُّسْفِرَةٌ (٣٨)
Some faces will be bright on that day, (38)
ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ (٣٩)
laughing and joyous, (39)
وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَلَيْهَا غَبَرَةٌ (٤٠)
and some faces on that day will be covered with dust, (40)
تَرْهَقُهَا قَتَرَةٌ (٤١)
overcast with gloom. (41)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَفَرَةُ ٱلْفَجَرَةُ (٤٢)
It is they who are the faithless, the vicious. (42)

Commentary

The Shout of Resurrection

After mentioning a significant portion of Divine favors and worldly blessings, the text turns to the description of the Hereafter and a glimpse of the events of Resurrection and the fate of the believers and disbelievers. This is done, on one hand, to declare that these favors and enjoyments, whatever they may be, are fleeting and have an end point, and on the other hand, the existence of these things is a proof of Allah's power over all things and the reality of the Resurrection.

It says: "When that deafening and ear-splitting cry (the Shout of Resurrection) comes, the disbelievers and criminals will fall into deep sorrow and regret" (فَإِذا جاءَتِ الصَّاخَّةُ) [1]

[1]: Regarding what the "result" (jaza) of the "condition" (shart) is in this conditional sentence, several possibilities exist: First, that it is omitted and understood from the subsequent verses, as if it were: "When the Deafening Cry comes, how great will be the grief of the disbelievers" (Tafsir al-Maraghi). Some have said the result is the sentence "Every man, that Day, will have a matter to occupy him" (Majma' al-Bayan). It has also been suggested that the result is derived from the phrase "The Day a man shall flee," as if it were: "When the Deafening Cry comes, a man shall flee from his brother" (Ruh al-Ma'ani).

"Sakhkhah" (الصَّاخَّةُ) is derived from the root "sakhkh," which originally means a sound so intense that it nearly deafens a person, or actually does deafen the ear. Here, it refers to the "Second Blowing of the Trumpet," that great shout which is the cry of awakening and life, resurrecting everyone and summoning them to the arena of Gathering.

Indeed, this shout is so great and soul-shaking that it makes man oblivious to everything except himself, his deeds, and his destiny.

And therefore, immediately after that, it adds: "On that Day, a man shall flee from his brother" (يَوْمَ يَفِرُّ الْمَرْءُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ‌). The same brother who was as dear as his own soul, whom he remembered everywhere and thought of constantly—today, he completely flees from him.

And likewise "from his mother and his father" (وَ أُمِّهِ وَ أَبِيهِ).

"And his wife and his children" (وَ صاحِبَتِهِ وَ بَنِيهِ). In this manner, man not only forgets his closest relatives—brother, father, mother, wife, and children—but actually flees from them. This indicates that the horror and terror of the Gathering are so immense that they sever man from all his bonds and affections.

The mother he loved dearly.

The father he deeply respected.

The spouse for whom he had intense affection.

And the children who were considered the fruit of his heart and the light of his eyes.

Some have said the meaning is fleeing from brothers, parents, spouses, and children who did not follow the path of faith, piety, and obedience to Allah; he flees from them lest he be afflicted with their fate.

Others have said this flight is because he fears they might have rights over him and demand them from him, while he is unable to fulfill them.

Among these three interpretations, the first seems more appropriate, though there is no obstacle to combining them.

As for why the verse begins with the brother, then the mother, then the father, then the wife, and finally the children, some believe it is because it moves from a lower level to a higher level of attachment. Eloquence dictates starting with the brother, then the parents, and finally the wife and children.

However, considering that not all people are identical in their affection and relationship with these five groups—sometimes a brother plays a more important role in a person's life and is more beloved, sometimes the spouse, and sometimes the child—one cannot provide a universal rule here.

Of course, much can be said about the importance of man's bond with each of these five groups, but it is not such that one can absolutely prefer one over the other in all respects—especially in the form presented in the verse. Therefore, the above order is not necessarily based on a hierarchy of importance.

In the next verse, the reason for this flight is stated: "For every man, that Day, will have a matter to occupy him" (لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ‌). The expression "yughnihi" (makes him self-sufficient/occupies him) is a subtle metaphor for the fact that on that Day, man is so preoccupied with himself that he does not attend to anyone else. The events are so intense and terrifying that they are sufficient to occupy his entire mind and heart.

In a hadith, it is narrated that some of the family of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked him whether a person remembers his close friend on the Day of Resurrection?

He replied: "There are three places where no one remembers anyone: at the Scale (Mizan) until he sees whether his scale is heavy or light; at the Bridge (Sirat) until he sees whether he crosses it or not; and at the Scrolls (Suhuf) until he sees whether he receives his scroll in his right hand or his left. In these three places, no one remembers his intimate friend, his beloved, his relative, his sincere friend, his children, or his parents. And that is the word of Allah the Exalted: 'For every man, that Day, will have a matter to occupy him.'" [1]

Then, describing the state of the believers and the disbelievers on that Day, it says: "Some faces, that Day, will be bright and radiant" (وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُسْفِرَةٌ).

"Laughing and rejoicing" (ضاحِكَةٌ مُسْتَبْشِرَةٌ).

"And other faces, that Day, will be dust-stained" (وَ وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَلَيْها غَبَرَةٌ).

"Covered with darkness and gloom" (تَرْهَقُها قَتَرَةٌ).

"Those are the disbelievers, the wicked!" (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ). "Musfirah" (مُسْفِرَةٌ) is from the root "isfar," as we have mentioned before, meaning to become manifest and to shine, like the breaking of the dawn at the end of a dark night.

"Ghabarah" (غَبَرَةٌ) is from "ghubar," meaning the residue of dust that has risen from the ground and settled on something.

"Qatarah" (قَتَرَةٌ) is originally from "qatar," meaning the smoke that rises from wood or something else. Some linguists have also interpreted it as dust, but the combination of these two expressions in the above verses shows that these two words have two different meanings.

"Kafarah" (كَفَرَةٌ) and "Fajarah" (فَجَرَةٌ) are the plurals of "Kafir" and "Fajir." The first refers to those with corrupt beliefs, and the second refers to those with corrupt actions.

From these verses, it is clearly understood that on the scene of Resurrection, the effects of human beliefs and evil deeds will be manifest in their faces.

Furthermore, the mention of "faces" (وجوه) is because the color of the face, more than anything else, can express internal states—both mental and spiritual distress as well as physical suffering.

In any case, one group there will be laughing and rejoicing; their faces will be open and radiant. The light of faith and the purity of action will surge in their countenances, and "the color of their faces tells of their inner secrets."

Conversely, there is a group whose darkness of disbelief and the ugliness of their deeds are manifest in their faces, as if black dust has settled on them and a halo of smoke has enveloped them. Signs of grief, sorrow, hardship, and pain pour from their faces. In principle, as stated in Surah Ar-Rahman, verse 41: "The criminals will be known by their marks" (يُعْرَفُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيماهُمْ‌). On that Day, the color of the faces is sufficient to identify people.

Key Points The Path of Self-Building

The expressions used in the short and resonant verses of this Surah provide a comprehensive program for self-building:

  1. On one hand, it commands man to return to the beginning of his creation to break his pride and arrogance, and to see how he was created from a worthless drop of fluid. We know that one of the greatest obstacles on the path of self-building is this very pride and arrogance.

  2. On the other hand, it introduces Divine guidance as the best provision for this path, whether it be guidance originating from revelation and the instructions of the Prophets and Awliya (Saints), or guidance obtained through reason and the study of the laws and systems of the created universe.

  3. Then, it commands man to look well at his physical food, to open his eyes and see how this Merciful and Compassionate Creator has created various types of nutrients, grains, and fruits for him from this dark soil, and to bow in submission before His Lordship. He should not only look at the physical structure of these foods but also consider how they are acquired, for pure and lawful (halal) food is one of the important foundations of self-building.

  4. By greater reason, he must look at his spiritual food—from which source has it flowed? A pure source or a contaminated one? For unhealthy teachings and misleading propaganda are like poisoned food that endangers man's spiritual life.

It is strange that some people are very strict and meticulous regarding physical food, yet indifferent toward their spiritual food. They read any corrupt or corrupting book, listen to any kind of misleading teachings, and set no conditions or limits for their spiritual nourishment.

In a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Why is it that I see people, when food is brought to them at night, they take the trouble to light lamps so they can see what they are putting into their bellies, yet they do not care about the nourishment of the soul, by lighting the lamps of their intellects with knowledge, so they may remain safe from the consequences of ignorance and sins in their beliefs and actions?" [1]

A similar sentiment was expressed by his son, Imam al-Mujtaba عليه السلام: "I wonder at those who think about what they eat, yet do not think about what they perceive (intellectually). They keep away from their bellies what harms them, but they fill their chests with what destroys them." [2]

  1. Afterward, he should remember that the deafening Shout of the Gathering will wake everyone from the sleep of death and place man before his deeds. The conditions of the Gathering are so terrifying that man forgets his dearest loved ones. He must reflect: is he doing something today so that on that Day he may have a laughing, rejoicing, and radiant face, or an ugly, frowning, and dark one? He should prepare himself for that Day starting now.

O Allah! Grant us the success of self-building.

O Lord! Do not deprive us of life-giving spiritual nourishment.

O God! Wake us from our heavy sleep before the Shout of the Gathering.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah 'Abasa.

[1] and [2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 84, root "ta'am."
